sample.title: Love in the Time of Cholera
sample.plot: In Colombia just after the Great War, an old man falls from a ladder; dying, he professes great love for his wife. After the funeral, a man calls on the widow - she dismisses him angrily. Flash back more than 50 years to the day Florentino Ariza, a telegraph boy, falls in love with Fermina Daza, the daughter of a mule trader. Ariza is persistent, writing her constantly, serenading, speaking poetically of love. Her father tries to keep them apart, and then, one day, she sees this love as an illusion. She's soon married to Urbino, a cultured physician, and for years, Ariza carries a torch, finding solace in the arms of women, loving none. After Urbino's fall, are Ariza's hopes delusional?

sample.title: In the Fire
sample.plot: A doctor travels to a remote plantation to care for a disturbed boy who has inexplicable abilities. She ignites a war of science versus religion with the local priest who believes the boy is possessed by the Devil.

sample.title: We're No Angels
sample.plot: At Christmas, three prisoners, Joseph (Humphrey Bogart), Albert (Aldo Ray), and Jules (Sir Peter Ustinov) escape from Devil Island to a French small coastal town. They decide to rob a store, to get some money and clothes and travel by ship to another place. They pretend to be there to fix the roof, but pretty soon they realize that the financial condition of the family Ducotel is not good. André Trochard (Basil Rathbone), the selfish and mean owner of the establishment, exploits the Ducotel family. The three convicts spend Christmas night with the Ducotels and are so well treated by the family, that they decide to help them. Their pet will help them to fix the situation.

sample.title: The Naked Jungle
sample.plot: It's 1901. At 19, tough, stubborn Christopher Leiningen came to South America and built levees to claim thousands of acres of Rio Negro river land for a chocolate plantation. Now 34, with no knowledge of women, he recruits a mail-order bride in New Orleans. She's beautiful, independent, and arrives ready to be his stalwart helpmate; however, no one has told him she's a widow. He rejects her. During the next week, as she awaits the boat to take her back to the US, they learn that legions of army ants will strike in a few days' time. She joins the fight to save the plantation; their courage and his probable loss of all he's worked for may crack his resolve to send her away.

categories.label: river ports and coastal towns tied to steamship travel
categories.description: Movement of people, letters, and goods depends on navigable rivers and seacoast shipping, which bind distant settlements into wider trade and social worlds. What you'll see: wharves, passenger steamers, loading docks, harbor streets, and arrivals marked by trunks, cargo, and waiting crowds.

categories.label: plantation estates commanding land and labour
categories.description: Great estates devoted to export crops give local masters power over land, work, and the rhythm of surrounding life. What you'll see: plantations cut from jungle, overseers and hired hands, storehouses, levees and drainage works, and houses set apart from the workers' quarters.

categories.label: telegraph lines and letters carrying courtship
categories.description: Fast wires and slow post sit side by side, letting private feeling and public news travel farther than before without erasing distance. What you'll see: telegraph offices, handwritten notes, couriers, formal calling rituals, and anxious waits for messages to arrive.

categories.label: doctors, priests, and healers contesting authority
categories.description: Learned medicine gains prestige, yet spiritual explanations still command deep loyalty, so sickness and strange behavior become grounds for struggle over who may name the truth. What you'll see: bedside examinations, church rites, arguments over possession and diagnosis, and villagers measuring science against faith.

categories.label: Catholic morality shaping family and reputation
categories.description: Marriage, widowhood, sexuality, and proper conduct are judged within a moral order strongly marked by the Church and by public honor. What you'll see: funerals, priests at moments of crisis, chaperoned visits, scandal whispered through town, and families guarding a daughter's good name.

categories.label: old families and newcomers chasing respectability
categories.description: Status rests on lineage, manners, and education, but traders, immigrants, and self-made men try to buy entry into respectable society. What you'll see: careful dress, formal parlors, marriage bargaining, social slights, and households displaying refinement as proof of rank.

categories.label: penal colonies and exile at the empire's edge
categories.description: Remote coastal settlements serve as places of confinement and banishment, where imperial authority is felt through isolation, guards, and the difficulty of escape. What you'll see: prison islands, convicts under watch, small frontier towns living beside punishment, and ships as the only road in or out.

categories.label: jungle, river, and climate testing settlement
categories.description: Human schemes for order remain fragile before flood, heat, insects, disease, and dense tropical growth that can overrun fields and buildings. What you'll see: oppressive humidity, muddy banks, swarming pests, fever fears, jungle cleared by force, and nature pressing against every boundary.

categories.label: foreign capital and Atlantic ties steering commerce
categories.description: Local life is bound to overseas markets, imported goods, and outside powers through shipping, language, and the sale of tropical produce. What you'll see: merchants dealing in export crops, European fashions and manners, foreign buyers, port warehouses, and talk of routes beyond the continent.

categories.label: households run by servants and strict etiquette
categories.description: Domestic life in prosperous homes depends on servants, visiting customs, and clear rules about who may enter which spaces and on what terms. What you'll see: large dining rooms, household staff at work, formal meals, calling cards, verandas and courtyards, and constant attention to manners.
